# Selecting effective plant species for the phytoremediation of persistent organic pollutants and multielement contaminated fibrous sediments

[*Environmental Sciences Europe*](/)
**volume 37**, Article number: 117 (2025)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

284 Accesses

[Metrics details](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01168-9/metrics)

## Abstract

### Background

Wastewater discharges from the old pulp and paper industry led to the accumulation of contaminated wood pulping fibers and debris—referred to as fiberbanks (FB)—in the Baltic Sea and freshwater bodies across Sweden and other pulp-producing countries. These anthropogenic sediments are polluted with toxic metal(oid)s and persistent organic pollutants, and their decomposition releases greenhouse gases. Phytoremediation offers a nature-based solution for the ex-situ treatment of these fibrous sediments, but they present unique challenges due to the abundant and unstable organic matter and aged pollution. This study aims to identify potential plant candidates and to address the limitations of fiberbanks as a plants growing media for phytoremediation.

In a greenhouse experiment, we assessed the performance of five plant species (*Brassica juncea, Brassica napus, Helianthus annuus, Hordeum vulgare*, and *Poa annua*) grown in substrates formulated with fiberbank. The evaluation included plant growth parameters, bioconcentration and uptake efficiency of metal(oid)s (V, Cr, Co, Ni, Cu, Zn, As, Cd, and Pb), and the degradation of polycyclic and linear hydrocarbons.

### Results

Despite initial concerns, fiberbanks displayed favorable physical characteristics and a degree of fertility conducive to plant growth. Even though all tested species seeds could cope with fiberbanks acute toxicity, *H. vulgare* and *P. annua* showed better tolerance to the fiberbanks substrates and superior aerial biomass development, which promoted a highest toxic metal(oid)s uptake efficiency, regardless of lower bioconcentration factors for most of the target elements. Zn (17.16–23.25 mg/kg of FB), Cu (4.18–6.48 mg/kg of FB) and Cr (1.05–1.36 mg/kg of FB) were most effectively taken up by these plants. The uptake of Co (0.04–0.18 mg/kg of FB) and Ni (0.05–0.17 mg/kg of FB) was lower. As (0.01–0.02 mg/kg of FB), Cd (0.02–0.06 mg/kg of FB), Pb (0.02–0.04 mg/kg of FB) and V (0.02–0.03 mg/kg of FB) phytoextraction was not significant. None of the species exhibited a significant removal of targeted organic pollutants.

### Conclusions

Phytoremediation, either on its own or in combination with other strategies, shows promise for the remediation of fiberbanks. However, further research is needed to understand how the organic matrix and long-term underwater aging of fiberbanks affect pollutants bioavailability.

## Background

Until more stringent regulations emerged in 1969, unregulated wastewater discharges from the old Swedish pulp and paper industry led to the creation of deposits of polluted fibrous sediments on the Baltic Sea floor and in lakes and rivers across central and northern parts of Sweden. Fibrous sediments include fiberbanks (as usually referenced in the Scandinavian literature), which are deposits that can have several meters of accumulated lignocellulosic fibers and wood chips or sawdust residues, and the fiber-rich sediments located around them [[4](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01168-9#ref-CR4 "Apler A, Nyberg J, Jönsson K, Hedlund I, Heinemo SÅ, Kjellin B (2014) Fiberbanksprojektet—kartläggning av fiberhaltiga sediment längs västernorrlands kust. Geol Surv Sweden, Rep. 16:178"), [42](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01168-9#ref-CR42 "Norrlin J, Josefsson S, Larsson O, Gottby L (2016) Kartläggning och riskklassning Av fiberbankar i Norrland. SGU-Rapport 21:765")]. These anthropogenic, organic-rich sediments are typically mixed contaminated with metal(oid)s of high environmental concern and with persistent organic pollutants (POPs), like polychlorinated biphenyls (PCBs), chlorinated pesticides (such as DDTs and HCB) and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs), originated from the wood pulping processes and other associated factories situated nearby the pulping mills.

Approximately 10.8 million tons of contaminated fiber material were discharged into Sweden’s aquatic environment over the past century, resulting in fiberbanks covering an estimated area of 2.5 million m2 and for fiber-rich sediments about 26.5 million m2 across seabeds, rivers, and lakes in Sweden’s northern counties [[4](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01168-9#ref-CR4 "Apler A, Nyberg J, Jönsson K, Hedlund I, Heinemo SÅ, Kjellin B (2014) Fiberbanksprojektet—kartläggning av fiberhaltiga sediment längs västernorrlands kust. Geol Surv Sweden, Rep. 16:178"), [42](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01168-9#ref-CR42 "Norrlin J, Josefsson S, Larsson O, Gottby L (2016) Kartläggning och riskklassning Av fiberbankar i Norrland. SGU-Rapport 21:765")]. Similar issues are reported in Finland, Norway, Canada, and the USA, highlighting that polluted fibrous sediments are a widespread, yet insufficiently addressed environmental concern [[15](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01168-9#ref-CR15 "Colodey AG, Wells PG (1992) Effects of pulp and paper mill effluents on estuarine and marine ecosystems in Canada: a review. J Aquat Ecosyst Stress Recov 1:201–226. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/BF00044716
                  
                "), [29](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01168-9#ref-CR29 "Göransson G, Apler A, Dahlberg AK, Löfroth H, Josefsson S, Wiberg K, Frogner-Kockum P, Nylander P, Hedfors J, Snowball I (2021) Assessing the risk of contaminant dispersion from fibrous sediments of industrial origin. Front Marine Sci 8:1294. 
                  https://doi.org/10.3389/fmars.2021.729243
                  
                "), [33](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01168-9#ref-CR33 "Hoffman E, Alimohammadi M, Lyons J, Davis E, Walker TR, Lake CB (2019) Characterization and spatial distribution of organic-contaminated sediment derived from historical industrial effluents. Environ Monit Assess 191(9):590. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s10661-019-7763-y

                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.watres.2018.01.041
                  
                ")]. Located mostly in shallow waters, these sediments are vulnerable to resuspension through natural or anthropogenic process, which can increase pollutant dispersion and pose ecological and human health risks, especially near urban or recreational zones [[18](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01168-9#ref-CR18 "Dahlberg AK, Apler A, Frogner-Kockum P, Göransson G, Snowball I, Wiberg K, Josefsson S (2021) Dispersal of persistent organic pollutants from fiber-contaminated sediments: biotic and abiotic pathways. J Soils Sediments 21(4):1852–1865. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s11368-020-02871-1
                  
                "), [29](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01168-9#ref-CR29 "Göransson G, Apler A, Dahlberg AK, Löfroth H, Josefsson S, Wiberg K, Frogner-Kockum P, Nylander P, Hedfors J, Snowball I (2021) Assessing the risk of contaminant dispersion from fibrous sediments of industrial origin. Front Marine Sci 8:1294. 
                  https://doi.org/10.3389/fmars.2021.729243
                  
                ")]. Furthermore, the anoxic conditions within fiberbanks promote methylmercury formation [[50](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01168-9#ref-CR50 "Regnell O, Elert M, Höglund LO, Falk AH, Svensson A (2014) Linking cellulose fiber sediment methyl mercury levels to organic matter decay and major element composition. Ambio 43(7):878–890. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s13280-013-0487-2
                  
                ")] and the emission of greenhouse gases like methane and carbon dioxide, with an estimated release from Swedish fiberbanks potentially accounting for up to 7% of the country’s total known GHG emissions in 2021 [[38](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01168-9#ref-CR38 "Lehoux AP, Isidorova A, Collin F, Koestel J, Snowball I, Dahlberg AK (2021) Extreme gas production in anthropogenic fibrous sediments: an overlooked biogenic source of greenhouse gas emissions. Sci Total Environ 781:146772. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2021.146772

Metal(oid)s (such as Pb, Cd, Cr, Cu, Ni, Zn, Co, Ba, Hg or As), were released in the wastewater together with by-products from the pulping processes, such as pyrite ashes from the nowadays outdated sulfite-cooking process. Mercury, for instance, was used as a biocide and a catalyst in the pulp bleaching. PCBs were used in materials associated with the carbonless copy paper production. DDTs and HCBs were widely employed as insecticides and fungicides in the forest industry until banned in Sweden in the 1970s. The PAHs presence in fiberbanks can be attributed to fuel leakages from the factory facilities, but also to other varied sources since they are formed out of incomplete combustion of organic materials. Other high concern pollutants such as polychlorinated dioxins and furans originated from wood preservatives, can also be tracked down in fiberbanks samples [[5](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01168-9#ref-CR5 "Apler A, Snowball I, Josefsson S (2020) Dispersal of cellulose fibers and metals from contaminated sediments of industrial origin in an estuary. Environ Pollut. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.envpol.2020.115182

                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpolbul.2019.110750
                  
                ")].

Management alternatives for these sediments are currently under discussion, and include monitored natural attenuation, in situ capping [[19](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01168-9#ref-CR19 "Dahlberg A-K, Wiberg K, Snowball I, Lehoux AP (2023) Capping fiberbank sediments to reduce persistent organic pollutants (POPs) fluxes: a large-scale laboratory column experiment. Environ Pollut 333(June):122019. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.envpol.2023.122019
                  
                ")] or careful dredging and ex situ treatment [[32](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01168-9#ref-CR32 "Haller H, Paladino G, Dupaul G, Gamage S, Hadzhaoglu B, Norström S, Eivazi A, Holm S, Hedenström E, Jonsson A (2021) Polluted lignocellulose-bearing sediments as a resource for marketable goods—a review of potential technologies for biochemical and thermochemical processing and remediation. Clean Techn Environ Policy 25:409–425. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s10098-021-02147-3
                  
                ")]. In this last scenario, before the contaminated material is deposited on shore, proper ex situ treatment alternatives must have been assessed, since these sediments present unique characteristics linked to their high organic matter content and mixed variable pollution pattern. Phytoremediation strategies are nature-based solutions that tend to be more sustainable than physical and chemical techniques since they can often be carried out with less resource input and therefore be more cost-effective. Besides, they have better public acceptance and a smaller environmental footprint [[34](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01168-9#ref-CR34 "Hou D, O’Yonnor D, Igalavithana AD, Alessi DS, Luo J, Tsang DCW, Sparks DL, Yamauchi Y, Rinklebe J, Sk Y, Ok. (2020) Metal contamination and bioremediation of agricultural soils for food safety and sustainability. Nat Rev Earth Environ 1(7):366–381. 
                  https://doi.org/10.3390/microplastics1010007
                  
                ")]. Phytoremediation can be particularly effective for simultaneously degrading and removing organic and inorganic pollutants from a co-contaminated solid matrix, by means of phytoextraction and phytodegradation mechanisms within the plant tissue, or in the rhizosphere with the help of associated microbial communities (rhizodegradation) [[13](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01168-9#ref-CR13 "Chirakkara RA, Cameselle C, Reddy KR (2016) Assessing the applicability of phytoremediation of soils with mixed organic and heavy metal contaminants. Rev Environ Sci Bio Technol 15(2):299–326. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s11157-016-9391-0
                  
                "), [31](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01168-9#ref-CR31 "Haller H, Jonsson A (2020) Growing food in polluted soils: a review of risks and opportunities associated with combined phytoremediation and food production (CPFP). Chemosphere 254:126826. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.chemosphere.2020.126826
                  
                ")]. In addition, very little energy input is required, and plants can aid in sequestering carbon and be reused as a renewable energy source after harvesting [[41](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01168-9#ref-CR41 "Mench M, Schwitzguébel JP, Schroeder P, Bert V, Gawronski S, Gupta S (2009) Assessment of successful experiments and limitations of phytotechnologies: contaminant uptake, detoxification and sequestration, and consequences for food safety. Environ Sci Pollut Res Int 16(7):876–900. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s11356-009-0252-z
                  
                ")]. This makes the potential use of the technology more appealing and feasible especially for the Swedish fibrous sediments case, where the liability for environmental harm is not clear yet [[32](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01168-9#ref-CR32 "Haller H, Paladino G, Dupaul G, Gamage S, Hadzhaoglu B, Norström S, Eivazi A, Holm S, Hedenström E, Jonsson A (2021) Polluted lignocellulose-bearing sediments as a resource for marketable goods—a review of potential technologies for biochemical and thermochemical processing and remediation. Clean Techn Environ Policy 25:409–425. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s10098-021-02147-3
                  
                "), [60](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01168-9#ref-CR60 "Vázquez NE, Fernández Luqueño F, Peña Castro JM, Vera RI (2021) Coupling plant biomass derived from phytoremediation of potential toxic-metal-polluted soils to bioenergy production and high-value by-products-a review. Appl Sci (Switzerland). 
                  https://doi.org/10.3390/app11072982
                  
                ")].

The use of plants has been well studied as a biological technique for the remediation of contaminated soils and sediments [[28](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01168-9#ref-CR28 "Gerhardt KE, Gerwing PD, Greenberg BM (2017) Opinion: taking phytoremediation from proven technology to accepted practice. Plant Sci 256:170–185. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.plantsci.2016.11.016
                  
                ")].

However, there are currently no references available on the application of phytoremediation technologies to this specific type of fibrous sediment, with such a high content of organic matter. Moreover, this organic matter is still undergoing degradation, and the associated pollutants have experienced aging processes for nearly fifty years under water. Currently, there is very limited—or even no—information on how these conditions influence the bioavailability or chemical state of the target pollutants for any bioremediation purposes. On the other hand, physical and chemical properties of material batches for cleaning up could vary greatly, depending on whether it consists primarily of polluted lignocellulosic fibers or a mixture of fibers and mineralogenic sediments. In addition, the synergistic or antagonistic effects of mixed toxic metals and organic contaminants on plant growth and remediation efficiency can be variable [[11](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01168-9#ref-CR11 "Cao X, Cui X, Xie M, Zhao R, Lei Xu, Ni S, Cui Z (2022) Amendments and bioaugmentation enhanced phytoremediation and micro-ecology for Pahs and heavy metals co-contaminated soils. J Hazard Mater 426:128096. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jhazmat.2021.128096
                  
                ")], amplifying the challenge to create adequate conditions for plant growth and phytoremediation success in fibrous sediments.

The primary objectives of this study were: (i) to address the limitations of fiberbanks to be used as a plant growing media; (ii) to identify potential plant candidates suitable for fibrous sediments ex-situ phytoremediation; (iii) to assess the ability of selected plants to uptake target toxic metal(oid)s and degrade organic pollutants present in fiberbanks.

## Methods

### Fiberbanks sampling and pretreatment

Fiberbank material was collected from Sundsvalls Bay in the Gulf of Bothnia, Sweden. This area was historically affected by industrial activities, primarily from the Ortviken sulfite pulp mill factory, a nearby sawmill, and related activities. A 500 L bulk sample was collected using a two-meter-long core tube sampler at a water depth of 18 m. The sample was taken from the easternmost fiberbank in the bay (Fig. [1](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01168-9#Fig1)), which spans an area of 45,000 square meters and contains a 6 m-thick layer of waste cellulose fibers [[4](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01168-9#ref-CR4 "Apler A, Nyberg J, Jönsson K, Hedlund I, Heinemo SÅ, Kjellin B (2014) Fiberbanksprojektet—kartläggning av fiberhaltiga sediment längs västernorrlands kust. Geol Surv Sweden, Rep. 16:178")]. The collected fiberbank material dewatering was conducted for 5 days in a 1 m3-container equipped with gravel and a geotextile bed.

![figure 1](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1186%2Fs12302-025-01168-9/MediaObjects/12302_2025_1168_Fig1_HTML.png)

The Fiberbank sampled in Sundsvall´s Bay in the Bothnian Sea (WGS84: 62°23´32.5″ N, 17°23´25.3″ E), is indicated with the red circle

After dewatering, the material was air-dried under a fume hood at approximately 20 °C for 7 days, then ground and sieved through an 11 mm mesh. Representative samples of the fresh dewatered material, the air-dried fiberbank, and the runoff water were sent to an external laboratory (Eurofins Environment Testing Sweden AB) for a comprehensive environmental hazards screening covering 161 parameters. This screening included analysis for various pollutants such as aliphatic and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, volatile organic compounds (VOCs), phenols, organochlorine pesticides, PCBs, selected herbicides, methylmercury, and toxic metals and metalloids. During the dewatering process, there was minimal washing out of pollutants, with only around 10% of the original concentration of Benzene detected in the runoff water sample. The air-drying process did not significantly alter the concentrations of most contaminants, except for methylmercury, which decreased by approximately 50% in the dried material (Table SM1, Supplementary Material).

### Selected plants species and fiberbanks phytotoxicity

Five plant species were selected for the phytoremediation tests in fiberbanks based on the specific criteria: (i) the ability to accumulate multiple metals, (ii) extensive root systems for organic pollutants degradation, (iii) suitability for growth in Nordic climatic conditions, and (iv) potential for post-harvest biomass reuse. The chosen species were *Brassica juncea L.* (Indian or salad mustard) and *Brassica napus L.* (canola) from the Brassicaceae family; *Hordeum vulgare* (barley) and *Poa annua* (tall meadow or blue grass) from the Poaceae family; and *Helianthus annuus L.* (common sunflower) from the Asteraceae family.

Brassica species are known for accumulating Zn, Cu, Ni, Cd, and Pb [[9](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01168-9#ref-CR9 "Brunetti G, Farrag K, Soler-Rovira P, Nigro F, Senesi N (2011) Greenhouse and field studies on Cr, Cu, Pb and Zn phytoextraction by brassica Napus from contaminated soils in the Apulia Region, Southern Italy. Geoderma 160(3–4):517–523. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.geoderma.2010.10.023
                  
                "), [35](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01168-9#ref-CR35 "Ishikawa S, Noriharu AE, Murakami M, Wagatsuma T (2006) Is Brassica juncea a suitable plant for phytoremediation of cadmium in soils with moderately low cadmium contamination?—Possibility of using other plant species for Cd-phytoextraction. Soil Sci Plant Nutr 52(1):32–42. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1747-0765.2006.00008.x
                  
                "), [47](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01168-9#ref-CR47 "Purakayastha TJ, Viswanath T, Bhadraray S, Chhonkar PK, Adhikari PP, Suribabu K (2008) Phytoextraction of zinc, copper, nickel and lead from a contaminated soil by different species of Brassica. Int J Phytoremediation 10(1):61–72")]. Particularly, *B. juncea* varieties, have shown promise in Hg uptake [[45](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01168-9#ref-CR45 "Pedron F, Petruzzelli G, Barbafieri M, Tassi E, Ambrosini P, Patata L (2011) Mercury mobilization in a contaminated industrial soil for phytoremediation. Commun Soil Sc Plant Anal 42(22):2767–2777"), [49](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01168-9#ref-CR49 "Raj D, Kumar A, Maiti SK (2020) Brassica juncea (L.) Czern. (Indian mustard): a putative plant species to facilitate the phytoremediation of mercury contaminated soils. Int J Phytoremediation 22(7):733–744. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1080/15226514.2019.1708861
                  
                ")]. Postharvest uses for *B. juncea* include spice crop seeds and fodder, while *B. napus* oil serves as high-quality vegetable edible oil for various purposes. Poaceae grasses, are potential candidates for phytoextraction due to their extensive root systems, high biomass yields, fast growth rates and adaptations to harsh environments [[44](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01168-9#ref-CR44 "Patra DK, Acharya S, Pradhan C, Patra HK (2021) Poaceae plants as potential phytoremediators of heavy metals and eco-restoration in contaminated mining sites. Environ Technol Innov 21:101293. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eti.2020.101293
                  
                ")]. Particularly, *Poa annua* has demonstrated bioaccumulation abilities for Pb, Zn, Cd, As, and Hg [[6](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01168-9#ref-CR6 "Barbafieri M, Dadea C, Tassi E, Bretzel F, Fanfani L (2011) Uptake of heavy metals by native species growing in a mining area in Sardinia, Italy: discovering native flora for phytoremediation. Int J Phytorem 13(10):985–997. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1080/15226514.2010.549858
                  
                "), [16](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01168-9#ref-CR16 "Comino E, Fiorucci A, Menegatti S, Marocco C (2009) Preliminary test of arsenic and mercury uptake by Poa Annua. Ecol Eng 35(3):343–350. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecoleng.2008.09.017
                  
                ")]. Barley (*Hordeum vulgare*) has demonstrated the ability to uptake arsenic, which is usually a problematic target for phytoremediation trials [[39](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01168-9#ref-CR39 "Mains D, Craw D, Rufaut C, Smith C (2007) Phytostabilization of gold mine tailings, New Zealand. Part 1: plant establishment in alkaline saline substrate. Int J Phytoremediation 8(2):131–147. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1080/15226510600678472
                  
                ")]. Barley grains have several uses in food production, particularly in beer breweries. *Helianthus annuus*, showed to be able to cope with and uptake several toxic metals, exhibiting good bioconcentration factors (i.e. bioconcentration values close to or higher than 1) for Zn, Pb, and Cd [[12](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01168-9#ref-CR12 "Chen L, Yang JY, Wang D (2020) Phytoremediation of uranium and cadmium contaminated soils by sunflower (Helianthus annuus L.) enhanced with biodegradable chelating agents. J Clean Prod 263:121491. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2020.121491
                  
                "), [14](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01168-9#ref-CR14 "Chirakkara RA, Reddy KR (2015) Biomass and chemical amendments for enhanced phytoremediation of mixed contaminated soils. Ecol Engin 85:265–274. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecoleng.2015.09.029
                  
                ")]. The sunflower offer various postharvest uses, such as biomass for energy production or seeds for common edible oil extraction.

Seeds from selected plant species were obtained from local organic suppliers, checked for anomalies, and sieved for uniformity with no further pretreatment. Seeds viability testing showed over 70% germination potential in distilled water for all the plant species. A commonly used phytotoxicity test based on the Zucconi method [[20](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01168-9#ref-CR20 "Devesa-Rey R, Moldes AB, Díaz-Fierros F, Barral MT (2008) Toxicity of anllóns river sediment extracts using microtox and the zucconi phytotoxicity test. Bull Environ Contam Toxicol 80(3):225–230. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s00128-007-9350-0
                  
                "), [62](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01168-9#ref-CR62 "Zucconi F, Forte M, Monaco A, De Bertoldi M (1981) Biological evaluation of compost maturity. Biocycle 22(4):27–29")], was employed. After stabilization in the dark for 24 h, a suspension of 1:10 fiberbank/water ratio (referred to as “100% water-extract”) was filtered and diluted to 60, 30, and 10% with distilled water. Ten to twenty seeds of each selected plant species were incubated (7 days at 25 ± 2 °C) by quadruplicate in Petri dishes with 3 ml of each of the dilutions or with water for germination control. Germinated seeds (radicle length > 5 mm) were counted, and root elongation was measured. The Germination Index was calculated using the following Eq. ([1](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01168-9#Equ1)):

where Ge is the number of germinated seeds in the fiberbank extracts; Gc is the number of germinated seeds in distilled water; Le is the average value of root length in fiberbanks extracts in mm; and Lc is the average value of root length in the control plates (mm).

### Experimental design

A phytoremediation trial was conducted in a controlled greenhouse setting using a two-factor factorial design (i.e. substrate type and plant species). # Phytoremediation of pollutants in oil-contaminated soils by *Alhagi camelorum*: evaluation and modeling

[*Scientific Reports*](/srep)
**volume 14**, Article number: 5502 (2024)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

7799 Accesses

20 Citations

[Metrics details](/articles/s41598-024-56214-y/metrics)

### Subjects

## Abstract

Phytoremediation is a cost-effective and environmentally friendly method, offering a suitable alternative to chemical and physical approaches for the removal of pollutants from soil. This research explored the phytoremediation potential of *Alhagi camelorum*, a plant species, for total petroleum hydrocarbons (TPHs) and heavy metals (HMs), specifically lead (Pb), chromium (Cr), nickel (Ni), and cadmium (Cd), in oil-contaminated soil. A field-scale study spanning six months was conducted, involving the cultivation of *A. camelorum* seeds in a nursery and subsequent transplantation of seedlings onto prepared soil plots. Control plots, devoid of any plants, were also incorporated for comparison. Soil samples were analyzed throughout the study period using inductively coupled plasma-optical emission spectroscopy (ICP‒OES) for HMs and gas chromatography‒mass spectrometry (GC‒MS) for TPHs. The results showed that after six months, the average removal percentage was 53.6 ± 2.8% for TPHs and varying percentages observed for the HMs (Pb: 50 ± 2.1%, Cr: 47.6 ± 2.5%, Ni: 48.1 ± 1.6%, and Cd: 45.4 ± 3.5%). The upward trajectory in the population of heterotrophic bacteria and the level of microbial respiration, in contrast to the control plots, suggests that the presence of the plant plays a significant role in promoting soil microbial growth (*P* < 0.05). Moreover, kinetic rate models were examined to assess the rate of pollutant removal. The coefficient of determination consistently aligned with the first-order kinetic rate model for all the mentioned pollutants (R2 > 0.8). These results collectively suggest that phytoremediation employing *A. camelorum* can effectively reduce pollutants in oil-contaminated soils.

## Introduction

From a worldwide perspective, soil is recognized as the third vital component of the environment, after water and air. The presence of healthy soil is essential for life on Earth, as 95% of the food consumed by humans is derived from the soil. Planning for the preservation of healthy soil is crucial to ensure human survival. The introduction of pollutants into the soil leads to changes in soil quality and gives rise to environmental issues[1](/articles/s41598-024-56214-y#ref-CR1 "Chen, T. et al. Heavy metal sources identification and sampling uncertainty analysis in a field-scale vegetable soil of Hangzhou, China. Environ. Pollut. 157, 1003–1010 (2009)."). Environmental pollution, stemming from human activities and the release of pollutants, has given rise to a range of environmental issues. One notable problem among these is the contamination of soil with petroleum compounds in regions abundant in oil and in the vicinity of oil refineries[2](/articles/s41598-024-56214-y#ref-CR2 "Zhu, A., Liu, J., Qiao, S. & Zhang, H. Distribution and assessment of heavy metals in surface sediments from the Bohai Sea of China. Mar. Pollut. Bull. 153, 110901 (2020)."). Crude oil stands as one of the paramount energy sources globally, yet its extensive production, transportation, consumption, and disposal processes contribute significantly to large-scale environmental pollution[3](/articles/s41598-024-56214-y#ref-CR3 "Gitypour, S., Nabi Bidhendi, G. R. & Gorghi, M. Contamination of soils near the Tehran oil refinery by leakage of crude oil. Environ. Stud. 30, 39–45 (2004).").

In countries with ongoing exploration activities, facilities, refineries, and abundant oil resources, the leakage, seepage, and infiltration of petroleum pollutants and derivatives into the soil are regarded as some of the most substantial soil contaminants[4](/articles/s41598-024-56214-y#ref-CR4 "Jaskulak, M., Grobelak, A. & Vandenbulcke, F. Modelling assisted phytoremediation of soils contaminated with heavy metals–main opportunities, limitations, decision making and future prospects. Chemosphere 249, 126196 (2020)."). In these regions, there exists the potential for a substantial annual influx of hazardous pollutants into the environment, which may result in adverse effects on the ecosystem[5](/articles/s41598-024-56214-y#ref-CR5 "Perera, F. Pollution from fossil-fuel combustion is the leading environmental threat to global pediatric health and equity: Solutions exist. Int. J. Environ. Res. Public Health 15, 16 (2018)."). Factors such as drilling, the emission of pollutants from refineries and power plants, leakage from oil reservoirs, tanker accidents, and oil spills all contribute to the escalation of pollution issues in the surrounding soils of these areas[6](/articles/s41598-024-56214-y#ref-CR6 "Adesina, G. O. & Adelasoye, K. A. Effect of crude oil pollution on heavy metal contents, microbial population in soil, and maize and cowpea growth. Agricult. Sci. 2014 (2014).").

One category of pollutants introduced into the soil through crude oil comprise HMs[7](/articles/s41598-024-56214-y#ref-CR7 "Ahmadov, M. et al. Assessment of heavy metal pollution in coastal sediments of the western Caspian Sea. Environ. Monit. Assess. 192, 1–18 (2020)."). HMs stand out as the most significant soil pollutants, known for their adverse effects on human health and their potential to diminish both agricultural production and product quality[4](/articles/s41598-024-56214-y#ref-CR4 "Jaskulak, M., Grobelak, A. & Vandenbulcke, F. Modelling assisted phytoremediation of soils contaminated with heavy metals–main opportunities, limitations, decision making and future prospects. Chemosphere 249, 126196 (2020)."). While these metals naturally occur in soil in trace amounts and are deemed essential nutrients for plants in small quantities, elevated levels can lead to toxicity, posing hazards to both plant life and human well-being[8](/articles/s41598-024-56214-y#ref-CR8 "Nadouki, M., Saffari, V. & Sarcheshmeh Pour, M. Effect of Different Concentrations of Copper and Lead on Stomata Changes, Morphological and Biochemical Characteristics of Four O’Clock (Mirabilis jalapa). Isfahan Univ. Technol.-J. Crop Product. Process. 9, 61–74 (2019)."). HMs are biologically nondegradable, and owing to their extended half-life, they can exert long-term detrimental effects on the soil and its biological processes. Moreover, they have the potential to induce various diseases in humans. The nondegradability of HMs has positioned them as one of the most hazardous groups of environmental pollutants[2](/articles/s41598-024-56214-y#ref-CR2 "Zhu, A., Liu, J., Qiao, S. & Zhang, H. Distribution and assessment of heavy metals in surface sediments from the Bohai Sea of China. Mar. Pollut. Bull. 153, 110901 (2020).").

These Mineral salts can infiltrate aquatic ecosystems via wastewater discharge, sewage, rainfall runoff, and atmospheric deposition[4](/articles/s41598-024-56214-y#ref-CR4 "Jaskulak, M., Grobelak, A. & Vandenbulcke, F. Modelling assisted phytoremediation of soils contaminated with heavy metals–main opportunities, limitations, decision making and future prospects. Chemosphere 249, 126196 (2020)."). HMs exhibit a pronounced inclination to amass within the bodies of aquatic organisms, leading to their accumulation[7](/articles/s41598-024-56214-y#ref-CR7 "Ahmadov, M. et al. Assessment of heavy metal pollution in coastal sediments of the western Caspian Sea. Environ. Monit. Assess. 192, 1–18 (2020)."). By consuming these organisms, pollution permeates higher trophic levels and ultimately reaches humans, positioned at the apex of the food chain, posing a threat to their health[9](/articles/s41598-024-56214-y#ref-CR9 "Rasouli-Sadaghiani, M., Karimi, H., Ashrafi Saeidlou, S. & Khodaverdiloo, H. The effect of humic acid on the phytoremediation efficiency of Pb in the contaminated soils by wormwood plant (Artemicia absantium). JWSS-Isfahan Univ. Technol. 22, 261–278 (2019)."). The presence of HMs in environmental pollution can lead to various health repercussions, including strokes and heart attacks, cancers, heightened kidney issues, genetic disorders, mental and psychological disorders, the birth of infants with defects, diminished intelligence quotient, and a lack of concentration[10](/articles/s41598-024-56214-y#ref-CR10 "Yu, Z. et al. Comprehensive assessment of heavy metal pollution and ecological risk in lake sediment by combining total concentration and chemical partitioning. Environ. Pollut. 269, 116212 (2021).").

Another pollutant that infiltrates the soil through crude oil is petroleum hydrocarbon. Petroleum hydrocarbons are the most significant sources of environmental pollution on a global scale[11](/articles/s41598-024-56214-y#ref-CR11 "Jung, D., Kim, J.-A., Park, M.-S., Yim, U. H. & Choi, K. Human health and ecological assessment programs for Hebei Spirit oil spill accident of 2007: Status, lessons, and future challenges. Chemosphere 173, 180–189 (2017)."). Soil pollution with petroleum hydrocarbons is a major concern in areas involved in oil extraction. In recent decades, there has been significant attention given to the environmental impact of hydrocarbon pollution[5](/articles/s41598-024-56214-y#ref-CR5 "Perera, F. Pollution from fossil-fuel combustion is the leading environmental threat to global pediatric health and equity: Solutions exist. Int. J. Environ. Res. Public Health 15, 16 (2018)."). The existence of petroleum hydrocarbons in soil can adversely affect biological processes conducted by soil microorganisms, leading to toxicity. This issue can have detrimental effects on soil quality. Furthermore, these compounds can exert negative influences on the chemical properties of the soil[12](/articles/s41598-024-56214-y#ref-CR12 "Roy, A. S. et al. Bioremediation of crude oil contaminated tea plantation soil using two Pseudomonas aeruginosa strains AS 03 and NA 108. African J. Biotechnol. 12 (2013)."). Petroleum hydrocarbons are causing obstruction of pore spaces between soil particles, elevating soil temperature and diminishing the presence of beneficial microorganisms. Additionally, these pollutants impede the absorption of nutrients by plants, ultimately resulting in plant mortality[13](/articles/s41598-024-56214-y#ref-CR13 "Igoni, A. H. Understanding the Niger Delta Environment for Sustainable Agricultural Mechanization (Annual Lecture/Awards Ceremony of the Nigerian Society of Engineers, 2018)."). In recent years, innovative methods for bioremediation of soil pollutants have been researched and applied, yielding favorable results from these studies[14](#ref-CR14 "Guarino, C., Spada, V. & Sciarrillo, R. Assessment of three approaches of bioremediation (Natural Attenuation, Landfarming and Bioagumentation-Assistited Landfarming) for a petroleum hydrocarbons contaminated soil. Chemosphere 170, 10–16 (2017)."),[15](#ref-CR15 "Okparanma, R. N., Ukoha-Onuoha, E. & Ayotamuno, J. M. Predicting selected kinetic parameters of hydrocarbon interactions with alum-based water treatment residuals. Results Eng. 16, 100726 (2022)."),[16](/articles/s41598-024-56214-y#ref-CR16 "Wang, C. et al. Remediation potential of immobilized bacterial consortium with biochar as carrier in pyrene-Cr (VI) co-contaminated soil. Environ. Technol. 40, 2345–2353 (2019)."). Several chemical and physical methods exist to address HMs pollution in soil, such as incineration and solvent-based extraction. However, these approaches are often deemed less effective due to their high costs and incomplete removal of pollutants. Currently, there is a growing emphasis on biological methods for the removal of contaminants from soil[17](/articles/s41598-024-56214-y#ref-CR17 "García-Sánchez, M., Košnář, Z., Mercl, F., Aranda, E. & Tlustoš, P. A comparative study to evaluate natural attenuation, mycoaugmentation, phytoremediation, and microbial-assisted phytoremediation strategies for the bioremediation of an aged PAH-polluted soil. Ecotoxicol. Environ. Saf. 147, 165–174 (2018).").

Phytoremediation is affordable and biological process that utilizes plants to eliminate and break down pollutants, including HMs, petroleum compounds, and various other toxic organic substances, from contaminated soil[18](/articles/s41598-024-56214-y#ref-CR18 "Cameselle, C. & Gouveia, S. Phytoremediation of mixed contaminated soil enhanced with electric current. J. Hazard. Mater. 361, 95–102 (2019)."). This technology relies on the integration of plant activity and the microbial community to nourish, transfer, deactivate, and immobilize soil pollutants. Phytoremediation can address the removal of pollutants through five distinct forms: phytostabilization, phytoextraction, phytodegradation, rhizofiltration, and phytovolatilization. In addition to garnering public acceptance, this method represents a novel and sustainable approach that is both suitable and cost-effective, particularly for developing countries[19](/articles/s41598-024-56214-y#ref-CR19 "Prieto, M., Acevedo, S., Prieto, G. & González, N. T. Phytoremediation of soils contaminated with heavy metals. Biodivers. Int. J. 2, 362–376 (2018)."),[20](/articles/s41598-024-56214-y#ref-CR20 "Khan, M. et al. In situ phytoremediation of metals. Phytoremediation: In-situ Applications, 103–121 (2020).").

In oil-rich areas, it is crucial to identify plant species capable of remediating contaminated soils. Native plant species adapted to polluted area environments can offer practical phytoextraction potential, particularly plants that tolerate drought, salinity, and contamination. In this study, based on field observations and previous research, the *A. camelorum* plant was selected for the phytoremediation of TPHs and HMs. This plant, belonging to the *Fabaceae* family, is renowned for its deep-rooted nature and resilience to cold and drought conditions[21](/articles/s41598-024-56214-y#ref-CR21 "Zangoie, M., Parsa, S., Jami Al-Ahmadi, M. & Izanloo, A. Effect of irrigation water salinity and plant density on forage quality of two camelthorn (Alhagi camelorum Fisch.) ecotypes. Environ. Stresses Crop Sci. (2023)."), it flourishes particularly well in the southern regions of Iran. In recent years, numerous studies on phytoremediation have been conducted to eliminate pollutants from the soil. Nevertheless, most phytoremediation research has focused on controlled greenhouse studies utilizing artificial pollutant spiking rather than field assessments under natural conditions. Additionally, few studies have characterized the kinetics of pollutant removal to better gauge required remediation timescales. The aim of this study was to investigate these research gaps and assess the phytoremediation capacity of *A. camelorum* for TPHs and HMs in oil-contaminated soils in a field-scale level. Therefore, we pursued three main objectives: (i) the *A. camelorum's* impact on this pollutants removal, (ii) its effect on soil microbial activity, and (iii) investigation of kinetic modeling for the removal rate of pollutants. The findings from this field-realistic phytoremediation assessment, using pollution-resistant native species, can offer practical strategies for mitigating the risks of oil pollution.

## Materials and methods

### Study area

The present study was conducted in the southwest country of Iran and the oil-rich region of Lishter, located in Gachsaran oil field and in close proximity to oil wells with geographical coordinates of 30°28′06.7"N 50°30′54.9"E. The Gachsaran oil Field (Fig. [1](/articles/s41598-024-56214-y#Fig1)) stands as one of Iran's oldest and most renowned oil-industrial regions. Boasting reserves of approximately 52 billion barrels of crude oil, it ranks as the country's second-largest oil field, following the Ahvaz oil field. Positioned at an average elevation of 720 m above sea level, this field experiences a tropical climate. The surrounding region is home to a population exceeding 120,000 individuals. With an average annual temperature of 22.5 °C and an annual precipitation of 441 mm, freezing temperatures occur only two days a year. The vegetation cover in this area consists of both annual and perennial herbaceous species, as well as shrubby plants[22](/articles/s41598-024-56214-y#ref-CR22 "Zahermand, S., Vafaeian, M. & Bazyar, M. H. Ability of Alfalfa plant to refine soil contaminated with nickel and lead. J. Environ. Sci. Technol. 23, 1–14 (2021)."). Given the daily extraction of crude oil, coupled with refining and oil transportation operations, the soils in the vicinity of this area are susceptible to significant oil pollution. Additionally, the substantial population residing nearby, combined with agricultural activities and crop harvesting from lands in proximity, poses potential risks to human health.

![figure 1](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41598-024-56214-y/MediaObjects/41598_2024_56214_Fig1_HTML.png)

Location of the study area.

### Soil preparation and plant cultivation

The soil under investigation was collected from 12 points in an oil-rich area at a depth of 0–20 cm. To ensure soil homogeneity, a sieve with a mesh size of number 10 (equivalent to 2 mm openings) was employed. Following mixing, the soil was irrigated for two weeks until it reached field capacity moisture, and turning every three days. The research site was prepared and excavation, and its floor has been covered with a geomembrane. Subsequently, contaminated soils, with a height of 50 cm, were transferred to the research site and leveled and compacted into plots (100 × 100 cm). The seeds used in this study were sourced from the Research Institute of Forests and Rangelands of Iran. The plant seeds were germinated and nurtured in a greenhouse environment (a nursery) using uncontaminated soil. After one month, seedlings of similar sizes and visual characteristics (height of 14 cm) were harvested and transferred to the designated soil plots and watering and plant care were carried out. Given the resilience of this plant and its low water requirements for growth, as well as to align with the actual environmental conditions of the region, watering was performed only until the seedlings initiated growth. Three replicates were conducted for each plot, and control plots (without plant cultivation) were also considered.[23](/articles/s41598-024-56214-y#ref-CR23 "Jones, J. B. Laboratory guide for conducting soil tests and plant analysis. (No Title) (2017).").

### Phytoremediation experiment

This study spanned a duration of six months. At the end of each month (every 30 days), soil samples were collected from all plots (containing plants and controls), and measurements were taken for HMs, TPHs, and microbial respiration of the soil[23](/articles/s41598-024-56214-y#ref-CR23 "Jones, J. B. Laboratory guide for conducting soil tests and plant analysis. (No Title) (2017)."). Additionally, soil bacterial enumeration was performed at the beginning of the study, after 90 days, and after 180 days.

#### Soil sampling

Soil sampling was conducted using sterile spatulas at 10 points within each plot at a depth of 0–10 cm. Stones and foreign materials were removed, and after mixing and homogenizing the collected soil, 100 g of soil were transferred to the laboratory for analysis. All stages of sampling and sample preparation were carried out according to standard methods[24](/articles/s41598-024-56214-y#ref-CR24 "Testing, A. S. f. & Materials. (American Society for Testing and Materials)."). The samples were stored at 4 °C until the final analysis.

#### Measurement and analysis of TPHs

The Agilent GC‒MS Model 5975 and the QuEChERS (Quick, Easy, Cheap, Effective, Rugged, and Safe) technique were employed for the measurement of TPHs. The effectiveness of this method in extracting hydrocarbons and organic compounds from soil, plants, foods, and animal samples has been reported[25](/articles/s41598-024-56214-y#ref-CR25 "Ben Salem, F., Ben Said, O., Duran, R. & Monperrus, M. Validation of an adapted QuEChERS method for the simultaneous analysis of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, polychlorinated biphenyls and organochlorine pesticides in sediment by gas chromatography–mass spectrometry. Bull. Environ. Contam. Toxicol. 96, 678–684 (2016)."). To initiate the analysis, 10 g of the dried sample were placed in a 50 mL centrifuge tube and shaken for 1 min with 3 mL of water, followed by an another minute of shaking with 15 mL of dichloromethane. A mixture of salts (4 g MgSO4, 1 g sodium chloride (NaCl), 0.5 g disodium hydrogen citrate sesquihydrate, 1 g trisodium citrate dehydrate) was added to the sample and shaken for another minute. The sample was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 8 min and then transferred to a clean centrifuge tube. Following this, the sample was filtered using a 45 μm nylon filter disk. Subsequently, 300 μL of the sample was injected into the GC‒MS instrument with 700 μL of dichloromethane for analysis. The temperature was initially set at 50 °C for 1 min, then increased to 120 °C at a rate of 25 °C/min, further to 160 °C at a rate of 10 °C/min, and then to 240 °C at a rate of 6 °C/min. Finally, it reached 315 °C at a rate of 2 °C/min and held for 10 min. High-purity helium carrier gas was employed with a flow rate of 1.2 mL/min[26](/articles/s41598-024-56214-y#ref-CR26 "Urban, M. & Lesueur, C. Comparing d-SPE sorbents of the QuEChERS extraction method and EMR-lipid for the determination of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAH4) in food of animal and plant origin. Food Anal. Methods 10, 2111–2124 (2017).").

#### Measurement and analysis of HMs

The analysis of HMs (Pb, Cr, Ni, and Cd) was conducted following standard measurement methods, utilizing an OPTIMA 2100 DV ICP‒OES[27](/articles/s41598-024-56214-y#ref-CR27 "Federation, W. E. & Association, A. Standard methods for the examination of water and wastewater. American Public Health Association (APHA): Washington, DC, USA 21 (2005)."). Initially, samples were dried in an oven at 70 °C, sieved through a 2 mm mesh, and prepared for extraction. Subsequently, 15 mL of 4 N nitric acid was added to 2 g of dried and sieved soil, which was then placed in an oven at 60 °C for 20 h. The samples were subsequently brought to volume in a 50 cc clean flask and distilled twice with water. In the next step, the samples were filtered through cellulose acetate filter paper (23 μm) to prepare them for analysis using an ICP‒OES device.

#### Contaminant removal and degradation

The pollution removal efficiency was calculated as a percentage based on Eq. [1](/articles/s41598-024-56214-y#Equ1).

where R represents the efficiency of pollutant removal (%), Co is the initial pollutant concentration (mg/L), and Ct is the pollutant concentration at the end of the study (mg/L)[28](/articles/s41598-024-56214-y#ref-CR28 "Ekperusi, O. & Aigbodion, F. Bioremediation of petroleum hydrocarbons from crude oil-contaminated soil with the earthworm: Hyperiodrilus africanus. 3 Biotech 5, 957–965 (2015).").

### Soil microbial activity

This study did not involve any microbial inoculation. The investigation concentrated exclusively on assessing the plant's impact on the natural soil microbial population's activity, considering soil respiration and analyzing the count of heterotrophic bacteria.

To determine the total number of heterotrophic bacteria in the soil, 1 g of soil was poured into a test tube containing 9 mL of sodium chloride solution (9000 mg/L). The obtained mixture was diluted to create dilutions ranging from 10−1 to 10−8. Then, the diluted solutions were transferred to nutrient-rich agar medium. The plates were placed in an incubator at a temperature of 28 °C for 48 h. Subsequently, the formed colonies were counted. The microbial population according to Eq. [2](/articles/s41598-024-56214-y#Equ2) is expressed in colony-forming units (CFU) per g of soil[29](/articles/s41598-024-56214-y#ref-CR29 "Moreira, I. T. et al. Phytoremediation in mangrove sediments impacted by persistent total petroleum hydrocarbons (TPH’s) using Avicennia schaueriana. Marine Pollut. Bull. 67, 130–136 (2013)."). A colony counter device was used for counting bacterial colonies.

Microbial respiration was monitored over a six-month period at monthly intervals. The measurement of CO2 produced by microbial respiration was carried out using the residual sodium hydroxide (NaOH) titration method[30](/articles/s41598-024-56214-y#ref-CR30 "Black, C. A. Methods of Soil Analysis. Sponsored by the American Society of Agronomy and the American Society for Testing Materials. (American Society for agronomy, 1965)."). Initially, 25 g of each sample was placed in dedicated microbial respiration containers, and distilled water, up to 70% of the agricultural capacity, was added to each sample. In each container, an experimental tube containing 10 mL of 0.5 N NaOH was placed, and the container lids were tightly closed. Subsequently, the containers were placed in an incubator at a temperature of 25 °C. At the designated time intervals, the experimental tubes were extracted from within the microbial respiration containers, and their contents were transferred into Erlenmeyer flasks. Subsequently, 10 mL of 10% barium chloride and a few drops of phenolphthalein indicator were introduced to each sample, and the contents of the Erlenmeyer flasks were titrated with 0.25 N sulfuric acid. Finally, the quantity of carbon produced as CO2 was computed based on the amount of acid generated, according to Eq. [3](/articles/s41598-024-56214-y#Equ3).

Ct is the amount of carbon released due to microbial respiration (mg/kg), B is the volume of acid consumed for control samples (mL), S is the volume of acid consumed by the sample (mL), N is the normality of acid consumed, E is the carbon equivalent weight, W is the weight of dried oven soil (g), and 1000 is the conversion factor from soil to kg.

### Kinetic rate modeling

Kinetic modeling is a key factor in understanding the biological removal process, measuring the rate of environmental biodegradation, and developing effective remedies for crude oil-contaminated environments. Information related to the kinetics of soil bioremediation is of paramount importance, as it determines the concentration of residual pollution at any given time and enables the calculation of the time required for soil remediation. The pollutant removal rate using the kinetic equation based on the first-order rate[31](/articles/s41598-024-56214-y#ref-CR31 "Agarry, S., Aremu, M. & Aworanti, O. Kinetic modelling and half-life study on enhanced soil bioremediation of bonny light crude oil amended with crop and animal-derived organic wastes. J. Pet. Environ. Biotechnol. 4, 137 (2013)."), as described in Eq. [4](/articles/s41598-024-56214-y#Equ4), was examined.

where Ct represents the concentration of a parameter at time t (mg/L), C0 represents the initial concentration of the parameter at time t (mg/L), *k* is the first-order rate constant (day-1), and t represents time (days).

The half-life (t1/2) is the time needed for the pollutant concentration to decrease by half of its initial concentration. The value is as observed in Eq. [5](/articles/s41598-024-56214-y#Equ5).

### Statistical analysis

The data were analyzed using SPSS 22 (IBM). Descriptive statistics were employed to calculate the mean and standard deviation. The Independent Samples Test was used for comparing TPHs, HMs, microbial respiration, and microbial counts before and after plant cultivation, compared with the control group. Temporal changes in the mentioned variables were assessed using Repeated Measure test. The significance level of (*p* < 0.05) was considered.

Highlights
----------

*   •[Phytoremediation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/phytoremediation "Learn more about Phytoremediation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is an economical and sustainable technology for environmental cleanup. 
*   •Plant species known to utilize different phyto-mechanisms during phytoremediation of diverse pollutants has been presented. 
*   •Different aids to overcome the limitations of phytoremediation are described. 
*   •Strengths and limitations inherent with the phytoremediation technique is supported by recent research. 

Abstract
--------

With current intensive agriculture practices and industrialization, pollution of natural resources like land and water with heavy metals, organic pollutants, radionuclides, pesticides, and fertilizers has become a major concern. Phytoremediation is a cost-effective and environmentally friendly technique that utilizes plants to immobilize, uptake, reduce toxicity, stabilize, or degrade the compounds that are released into the environment from different sources. Studies have shown that heavy metals, organic contaminants, radionuclides, antibiotics, and pesticides can be remediated using plants. Though phytoremediation has been practiced since decades, it is still an emerging technology. This review article summarizes existing information and synthesizes the recent findings on plant species suitable for use in phytoremediation through utilizing different mechanisms, aids that can enhance the efficiency of phytoremediation processes, and strengths and limitations that comes with the application of this technique. Diverse plants remediate different pollutants at different rates through one or multiple mechanisms. The limitations of phytoremediation can be overcome by using several aids including natural and chemical amendments, genetic engineering and natural microbial stimulation. Given the low-cost of phytoremediation compared to conventional technology and sustainability associated with plants and use of renewable energy, phytoremediation can be a reliable solution for a sustainable and economical remediation of soil and water from the organic and inorganic pollutants.

1. Introduction
---------------

Rapid rise in global population accompanied by ensuing demands for food, water, energy, timber, and other ecosystem services has led to severe stresses on the [natural resources](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/natural-resources "Learn more about natural resources from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and the environment ([Kamran et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0141); [Saleem et al., 2020c](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0235); [Zaheer et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0298)). [Anthropogenic activities](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/anthropogenic-activity "Learn more about Anthropogenic activities from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") including the agricultural intensification practices ([Mózner et al., 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0192)), speedy urbanization, and rapid industrialization ([Cherniwchan,2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0049); [Wu et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0289)) are causing serious environmental pollution by [heavy metals](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/heavy-metal "Learn more about heavy metals from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (HMs) and metalloids ([Anyakora et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0015); [He et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0118); [Su,2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0261)), radionuclides ([Hu et al., 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0125); [Prakash et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0215)), organic compounds ([Afzal et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0003)), agrochemicals ([Malik et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0177); [Sunitha et al., 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0265)), and oil spills ([Ron & Rosenberg,2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0228)). Mining activities, disposal of effluents from industries and [residential areas](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/residential-area "Learn more about residential areas from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), heavy use of fertilizers and pesticides, and irrigation of contaminated water have led to contamination of soils ([Awad et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0020); [Tang et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0267); [Teng et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0270)). Mining leads to disturbance of many soil properties, including pH, electrical conductivity, and cation exchange capacity ([Saleem et al., 2020a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0233)). Consequently, HMs such as Copper (Cu), Lead (Pb), Zinc (Zn), Nickel (Ni), Cadmium (Cd), Chromium (Cr), Iron (Fe), Manganese (Mn) and organic pollutants such as hydrocarbons, aromatic compounds, phenols, organo-chlorinated compounds, pesticides have been found at elevated concentrations in the soil and sediments ([Gutiérrez-Ginés et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0111); [Liu et al., 2017b](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0169); [Song et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0254)).

HMs accumulation in soil affects [soil microbial population](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/soil-microbial-populations "Learn more about soil microbial population from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), processes and their activity ([Huang et al., 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0126); [Singh & Kalamdhad,2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0249)). The effects of HMs on microbial population changes and enzymatic activities are dependent upon the type of HMs and their chemical affinity ([Karaca et al., 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0142)). For examples, cadmium (Cd) affects [ureases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/urease "Learn more about ureases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), proteases and basic phosphatases ([Lorenz et al., 2006](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0170)); lead (Pb) affects [catalases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/catalase "Learn more about catalases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), ureases, [invertase](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/invertase "Learn more about invertase from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and [acid phosphatase](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/acid-phosphatase "Learn more about acid phosphatase from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Belyaeva et al., 2005](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0032)); Arsenic (As) affects [sulfatase](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/sulfatase "Learn more about sulfatase from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and phosphatase ([Speir et al., 1999](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0258)); and copper (Cu) restricts b-glucosidase ([Geiger et al., 1998](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0097)). High chromium (VI) concentration in soil exhibits distinct toxicity characteristics causing negative effects in soil microbial cell metabolism ([Huang et al., 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0126)).

The [ingestion](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/ingestion "Learn more about ingestion from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of food crops contaminated with HMs can lead to [bioaccumulation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/bioaccumulation "Learn more about bioaccumulation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of HMs and subsequent biomagnification along the higher [trophic levels](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/trophic-level "Learn more about trophic levels from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in the food chain can threaten both animal and human health ([Singh & Kalamdhad,2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0249)). HMs accumulation in human body can cause serious complications in human health such as Itai-itai disease due to chronic Cd exposure ([Pan et al., 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0205)); kidney dysfunction and anemia due to Zn ([Duruibe et al., 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0072)); [mucosa](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/mucous-membrane "Learn more about mucosa from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") corrosion, hepatic system failure and [central nervous system](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/central-nervous-system "Learn more about central nervous system from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") damage due to Cu ([Singh & Kalamdhad,2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0249)); [skin irritation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/skin-irritation "Learn more about skin irritation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and [nervous system](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/nervous-system "Learn more about nervous system from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") complications due to Ni ([Argun et al., 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0016)); cardiovascular problems and damage to nervous system due to Pb ([Kazemipour et al., 2008](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0144); [Singh & Kalamdhad,2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0249)); abortion, nervous breakdown and skin rashes due to Hg ([Duruibe et al., 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0072)); cancer due to Cr ([Zohdi et al., 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0304)); and carcinogenic and [immune system](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/immune-system "Learn more about immune system from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") dysfunction due to As ([Duruibe et al., 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0072)).

Among different organic contaminants, chlorinated solvents, petroleum hydrocarbons explosives ([Alkorta & Garbisu,2001](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0011)), polychlorinated biphenyls (PCBs), polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs), pyrene, and trinitrotoluene (TNT) ([Chekol & Vough,2001](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0045)) are major soil contaminants. Pesticides that often contaminate soils include [atrazine](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/atrazine "Learn more about atrazine from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [dieldrin](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/dieldrin "Learn more about dieldrin from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [hexachlorobenzene](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/hexachlorobenzene "Learn more about hexachlorobenzene from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), dichlorodiphenyltrichlorethane (DDT), and 1,2,4- trichlorobenzene ([Chiou et al., 2001](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0050)). These pollutants seriously affect the [soil biota](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/soil-biota "Learn more about soil biota from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), mineral nutrients availability, and soil productivity because of their persistence in soil for long period of time and toxicity.

Like soils, [water resources](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/water-resources "Learn more about water resources from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") have also been contaminated by different pollutants. Urbanization and industrialization activities release toxic heavy metals through the sewage, runoff and effluents contributing to largest share of factors for water contamination ([Masindi & Muedi,2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0182); [Parveen et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0206); [Saleem et al., 2020a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0233)). Over 80% of world's wastewater reach to environment without treatment causing nearly 58% of health issues like diarrhea, which is a major cause for early child dealth ([Connor et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0054)). Among metals, Cu, Ni, Pb, Cd, Cr, U, and Zn frequently contaminate water resources ([Prasad & Freitas,2005](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0216)). The HMs degrade the quality of water and can lead to water-borne health hazards through heavy metal accumulation in the food chain ([Wongsasuluk et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0287)). Apart from HMs, organic pollutants contaminate water resources either by erosion, seepage, sludge and effluents from municipal waste and industries, and runoff from agricultural practices ([Diez,2008](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0064)) and mining ([Tanhan et al., 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0268)). These contaminants can accumulate over time and negatively affect the aquatic as well as [terrestrial ecosystems](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/terrestrial-ecosystem "Learn more about terrestrial ecosystems from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages").

Remediation of the soil and water pollutants for sustaining ecosystem processes and functions is one of the major challenges our society [faces](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/face "Learn more about faces from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") today. Many physical, chemical, and biological approaches have been employed for remediating the environmental pollution, however, their applications are limited due to the cost and labor requirements, safety hazards, and risks to ecosystems ([Ali et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0010)). An alternative technique, [phytoremediation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/phytoremediation "Learn more about phytoremediation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), can be an effective technique and is gaining popularity, acceptance, and implementation.

Phytoremediation technique utilizes plants to remediate the contaminated media including soil and water. Phytoremediation is an economically and environmentally favorable technique as it utilizes [green plants](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/viridiplantae "Learn more about green plants from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to contain, sequester or detoxify contaminants from contaminated soil and water ([Ashraf et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0019)). Phytoremediation utilizes many mechanisms including degradation (rhizo-degradation, phytodegradation), accumulation (phytoextraction, rhizofiltration), dissipation (phytovolatilization), and immobilization (hydraulic control and phytostabilization) to degrade, remove, or immobilize the pollutants ([Pivetz,2001b](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0214)). Depending upon the contaminants, plants utilize one or more of these mechanisms to reduce their concentrations from soil and water. For examples, plants uptake and accumulate the HMs in their tissues ([Mahar et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0176)) and degrade the organic pollutants ([Saleem et al., 2020b](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0234)) reducing their toxicity from soil and water resources.

Different plant uses different methods or their combinations to remediate soil and water depending upon the contaminant types, forms, and media. Contaminated ground water can be remediated through phytodegradation, phytovolatilization, rhizo-filtration, rhizo-degradation and phytodegradation. Surface and wastewater contaminations can be treated by [rhizofiltration](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/rhizofiltration "Learn more about rhizofiltration from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), phytodegradation or rhizodegradation. Soil, sediments or sludges contaminations are remediated through phytoextraction, phytodegradations, phytostabilization, rhizodegradation or phytovolatilization.

Ideal plant species for phytoremediation purpose should be hardy in nature, high biomass-producing, tolerant to toxic effects of metals and contaminants, easy to cultivate, have high absorption capacity, and be non-attractive for herbivory ([Adesodun et al., 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0002); [Sakakibara et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0231); [Shabani & Sayadi,2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0243)). In many cases, plant species cannot perform well and need aids to enhance phytoremediation. Such aids include soil amendments like biochar ([Paz-Ferreiro et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0207)), Ethylene Diamine Tetraacetic Acid (EDTA) ([Shahid et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0244)), endophytic bacteria ([Afzal et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0003)), [Arbuscular mycorrhiza](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/arbuscular-mycorrhiza "Learn more about Arbuscular mycorrhiza from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Gaur & Adholeya,2004](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0095)) or even [transgenic plants](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/transgenic-plant "Learn more about transgenic plants from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Abhilash et al., 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0001); [Kawahigashi,2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0143)). They promote the phytoremediation process either by reducing the toxicity of pollutants or increasing the availability of pollutants or promoting the growth of plants.

Previous articles have provided information regarding techniques ([Jadia & Fulekar,2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0132); [Sharma & Pathak,2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0245); [Willey,2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0285)), plants used ([Banuelos et al., 1997](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0027); [Cheng,2003](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0047); [Pilon-Smits & Pilon,2002](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0211)), factors affecting the processes ([Antoniadis et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0014); [Sheoran et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0246)), aids or stimulators ([Coninx et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0053); [Hovsepyan & Greipsson,2005](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0123); [Ullah et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0273)) and are either specific to soil ([Belouchrani et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0031); [Romeh,2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0227); [Waigi et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0278)) or water resources ([Nazir et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0197); [Rezania et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0226); [Ugya & Imam,2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0272)). enriched_points.expanded_content: Field trials have demonstrated the efficacy of phytoremediation across diverse environments, addressing pollutants such as heavy metals, organic compounds, and petroleum hydrocarbons.
Various plant species like *Brassica juncea*, *Helianthus annuus*, and *Alopecurus pratensis* have shown potential in different soil types and pollution contexts.
Field trials revealed significant pollutant reductions, with studies indicating up to 53.6% removal of total petroleum hydrocarbons and substantial heavy metal uptake by selected plants.
Successful phytoremediation has restored ecosystem health, improved soil quality, and enhanced biodiversity by reintroducing native plant species and stabilizing contaminated soils.
Local communities have actively participated in phytoremediation projects, fostering awareness and support for environmental restoration, resulting in shared benefits and sustainable practices.
